stimulus: Studies show that people who eat breakfast daily have lower rates of obesity than those who skip breakfast. Therefore, eating breakfast causes people to maintain a healthy weight.
stem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the argument above?

choices.label: A
choices.text: People who eat breakfast tend to exercise more regularly.

choices.label: B
choices.text: People who already maintain healthy diets are more likely to eat breakfast consistently.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Breakfast foods are often higher in protein than other meals.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Obesity rates have risen in the past decade despite breakfast food marketing.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Some nutritionists recommend eating multiple small meals instead of three large ones.

explanation: B undermines causation by suggesting reverse causation: healthy habits (not breakfast itself) cause both regular breakfast eating and lower obesity.

stimulus: The city council argues that installing more streetlights in the downtown area will reduce nighttime crime. Crime statistics from neighboring cities show that areas with more streetlights tend to have fewer reported incidents after dark.
stem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the council's argument?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The neighboring cities have larger police forces than our city.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Criminals in studies consistently report that well-lit areas deter them from committing crimes.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Streetlight installation is expensive and requires ongoing maintenance.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Daytime crime rates are unaffected by streetlight availability.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Some crimes occur indoors where streetlights have no effect.

explanation: B provides a mechanism explaining why streetlights reduce crime, strengthening the causal claim.

stimulus: Senator Walsh has proposed a bill to increase funding for public libraries. But Walsh was once caught plagiarizing a college paper, so his views on the importance of education and reading cannot be trusted.
stem: The reasoning in the argument above is flawed because it

choices.label: A
choices.text: relies on a sample that is too small to support the conclusion

choices.label: B
choices.text: attacks the person making the argument rather than addressing the argument itself

choices.label: C
choices.text: assumes that what is true of the parts is true of the whole

choices.label: D
choices.text: draws a conclusion that goes beyond what the evidence supports

choices.label: E
choices.text: treats a necessary condition as if it were a sufficient condition

explanation: This is an ad hominem fallacy — the argument attacks Walsh's character instead of evaluating the library funding proposal on its merits.

stimulus: Researchers at Hartfield University found that students who take handwritten notes retain information better than those who type notes on a laptop. Therefore, all university students should be required to take handwritten notes during lectures.
stem: The argument above depends on which of the following assumptions?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Laptops cause students to become distracted during lectures.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Information retention is the most important goal of note-taking in university courses.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Handwriting is a skill that can be improved with practice.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The Hartfield University study used a representative sample of students.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Typed notes are easier to share with other students.

explanation: The argument requires that retention is the primary goal — otherwise better retention alone wouldn't justify mandating handwriting.

stimulus: No amphibian can survive indefinitely in a fully saltwater environment. All frogs are amphibians. The Pacific chorus frog has been observed living in coastal wetlands where salinity levels fluctuate significantly.
stem: Which of the following can be properly inferred from the statements above?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The Pacific chorus frog prefers saltwater environments to freshwater ones.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The Pacific chorus frog cannot survive in an environment of constant high salinity.

choices.label: C
choices.text: All animals found in coastal wetlands are amphibians.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Salinity fluctuation has no harmful effects on the Pacific chorus frog.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The Pacific chorus frog is the most salt-tolerant of all frogs.

explanation: Since all frogs are amphibians and no amphibian can survive indefinitely in full saltwater, the Pacific chorus frog cannot survive in constant high salinity.

stimulus: A major airline introduced a customer loyalty program that rewards frequent flyers with free upgrades and bonus miles. In the year following the program's launch, the airline's overall customer satisfaction scores dropped significantly, even though the number of passengers who flew the airline increased.
stem: Which of the following, if true, most helps to explain the paradox described above?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Competing airlines did not introduce similar loyalty programs that year.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The influx of new passengers seeking loyalty rewards led to overcrowded flights and reduced service quality for all travelers.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Frequent flyers who received upgrades reported higher satisfaction than before the program.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The airline increased ticket prices in the year the program was introduced.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Customer satisfaction surveys were redesigned to include new questions.

explanation: B explains why satisfaction dropped even as passengers increased: more passengers meant worse service, reducing overall satisfaction despite the loyalty program's appeal.

stimulus: Every student who passed the qualifying exam was admitted to the graduate program. Some students who were admitted to the graduate program received scholarships. No student who failed the qualifying exam received a scholarship.
stem: If the statements above are true, which of the following must also be true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Every student admitted to the graduate program passed the qualifying exam.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Some students who received scholarships passed the qualifying exam.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No student who passed the qualifying exam failed to receive a scholarship.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Most students in the graduate program received scholarships.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Students who received scholarships performed better on the qualifying exam.

explanation: Scholarship recipients were admitted; admitted students with scholarships must have passed the exam (since failing = no scholarship). So some scholarship recipients passed.

stimulus: A nonprofit organization claims that its after-school tutoring program improves academic performance. As evidence, they note that students who participated in the program for one semester showed an average grade improvement of half a letter grade.
stem: Which of the following would be most useful to know in order to evaluate the nonprofit's claim?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Whether the tutors in the program held advanced degrees in their subject areas

choices.label: B
choices.text: Whether students who did not participate in the program showed similar or greater grade improvements over the same period

choices.label: C
choices.text: How many students participated in the program during the semester studied

choices.label: D
choices.text: Whether the nonprofit charges a fee for its tutoring services

choices.label: E
choices.text: Whether the grade improvement persisted into the following semester

explanation: To evaluate the causal claim, we need a control group. If non-participants improved equally, the program may not deserve credit.

stimulus: All birds have feathers. Penguins are birds. Therefore, penguins have feathers.
stem: Which of the following arguments is most similar in its logical structure to the argument above?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Most mammals are warm-blooded. Dolphins are warm-blooded. Therefore, dolphins are mammals.

choices.label: B
choices.text: All contracts require consideration. This agreement requires consideration. Therefore, this agreement is a contract.

choices.label: C
choices.text: All prime numbers greater than two are odd. Seventeen is a prime number greater than two. Therefore, seventeen is odd.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No reptiles are warm-blooded. Snakes are not warm-blooded. Therefore, snakes are reptiles.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Some athletes are vegetarians. Marcus is an athlete. Therefore, Marcus is a vegetarian.

explanation: The original uses a valid syllogism: All A are B; X is A; therefore X is B. Answer C follows the identical valid structure.

stimulus: Alicia: The proposed downtown development will be good for the city because it will bring in tax revenue and create construction jobs. Marcus: Any short-term economic gains from the development will be outweighed by the long-term costs of increased traffic congestion and displacement of existing residents.
stem: The dialogue above provides the most support for the claim that Alicia and Marcus disagree about whether

choices.label: A
choices.text: the development will generate significant tax revenue for the city

choices.label: B
choices.text: construction workers will be hired from within the local community

choices.label: C
choices.text: the overall effect of the development on the city will be positive

choices.label: D
choices.text: traffic congestion is a serious problem in the downtown area

choices.label: E
choices.text: the city government has the authority to approve the development

explanation: Alicia says the development is good overall; Marcus says it is net harmful. They directly disagree about whether the net effect is positive.
